speaker: interviewer
text: This is Roll 1 with David Lipstadt. The date is October 27, 1996. Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. The survivor's name is David Lipstadt. The interviewer's name is Sue Steinberg. We are in Los Angeles, California, and the language will be English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Sue Steinberg. I'm here with David Lipstadt. Today's date is October 22nd, 1996. We're in Los Angeles, California, and this interview will be in English. OK. Could you tell us your name and spell it for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is David Lipstadt, L-I-P-S-T-A-D-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did you have a Hebrew name or any other names? It happens to be in Hebrew David also. OK. Your birth date.

speaker: interviewee
text: April 22nd, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's talk a little bit about Warsaw. What-- what-- what were some of your early memories of growing up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I had a pretty good childhood. Warsaw was a big city. It was over a million population. There was 350,000 Jews living in Warsaw at the time. We had a very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how shall I say it-- conscious sort of life. We knew who we are and what we are doing. We-- I started-- at five years old, I started to go to a Hebrew school. And it-- it was a Hebrew school, but it had a lot of emphasis on secular studies, so by the time I-- I went to-- to this school, I learned quite a bit, because I-- we started at 8:00 in the morning and stayed at school until 2:30. Then we started again at 4 o'clock, and we came home at 7:30. So it was a very busy day for us. The only day off was Saturday when we didn't go to school. We used to play a lot of soccer, used to have a lot of fun. Sometimes we didn't have a real ball, so we improvised.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took a lot of rags and-- [LAUGHS] and made a ball out of it. And that's how we kicked it around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One, her name is-- my older sister was Fela, F-E-L-A, and my younger sister was Lilka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get along with them? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I loved Lilka very much. I was eight years older, and she was a lovely girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- tell me about your father. Your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's-- father's name is Hersh.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a jeweler. We had a jewelry store on the same street where we lived, oh, about five or six houses between the apartment we lived in and the store. My father and my mother worked in the store. I used to go-- come there with what little time I had when I was young and drop into the store. And I learned quite a bit about jewelry and jewels.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Hena, H-E-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she helped with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: She helped a lot in the store, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious? Was your father Orthodox or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were traditional. We-- we celebrated all the holidays, of course. And-- we were very much interested in Judaism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any favorite holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Favorite holidays. Well, I loved Passover. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I loved [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what sort of things would your family do to celebrate? How would-- how would--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to come together and have special meals for the holidays. And we were free. We didn't do anything. We didn't work. We went to synagogue together. And I had some pretty good voice, and I sang in the choir. And my mother was very, very proud of me because of this. We used to meet after-- after the services. At-- at the-- at the end of the services, of course, my mother came out from-- women and men were separated, weren't sitting in the same pews. So when we met after the services on the street, we would comment how did you like the rabbi's sermon and how was the cantor and how was the choir, if everything was all right. And then we went home and had a festive meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you have like other people from the temple come too? Or was there a big group that would come to your house, or big family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't recall large people coming to us. On occasion, occasionally we had company, of course, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother keep kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would be-- you said a special meal. What sort of things would that include? What would that-- what sort of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were the old standbys, gefilte fish, which were made from scratch, nothing from a jar or a can. Everything has to-- had to be fresh. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we enjoyed it. We had a good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your mother worked, so your sisters helped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, she used to get a little help once in a while, not on a steady basis but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- Were most of your friends Jewish and from school, or did you have non-Jewish friends as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was very little contact with non-Jewish boys. [LAUGHS] As a matter of fact, the only time we had contact with them is when they beat us up. Especially after we had a game and we happened to be the winners, [LAUGHS] it was very hard to leave the stadium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Before Hitler, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is just in general they would do this.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a-- I remember a specific boy, Jan Rutkowski, every time he saw me I had to give him a dime. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened if you didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: He would probably beat me up, because he was stronger than I was and-- but I had rather pay him the dime and-- and have peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he live near you? Or was he-- he wasn't at your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know where he lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: He just came--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I wasn't really interested.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did most of the-- so most of the Jews stayed together and separate from the rest of the population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, there were specific areas where-- where the Jewish inhabitants were about 90% of the population in those sections. But-- but we were spread all over the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your neighborhood? What was your neighborhood like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our neighborhood was, I should say, mostly Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did everybody get along OK, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Except in the late '30s when the German propaganda was very strong and it didn't take much to entice the-- the Poles with anti-Semitism, because they-- they're very prone to it. So they-- they used to come, students, university students, on-- in big groups and start trouble in the Jewish section. So we had to fight back, and we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we will-- since the Jews were a majority there, we chased them out, and they wouldn't-- they wouldn't  come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this? You said it was in the late '30s when  this was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was in the late '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back just a little bit, because you had mentioned something earlier. Tell me about your bar mitzvah. What was your bar mitzvah like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Bar mitzvah was not a very big occasion like it's lately here in the United States. You celebrated. You went to synagogue. And you got called to the Torah, and this was it. This was-- There wasn't a big celebration. But I remember one incident, where I knew that my aunt was making a-- a pouch for the tallis. But she kept it a secret. It was supposed to be a secret. But I knew it and my little sister knew it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one Saturday afternoon, we went to visit. And I sat there for a while and talked. Especially my grandmother would say, well, sit down at the balcony. Why you wanna go? Sit here. We'll talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so little Lilka was five years old. So she said to me, [? Dudek-- ?] they used to call me [? Dudek-- ?] take the pouch and let's go. And she blew the whole story. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so they knew that you knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your aunt's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whose?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your aunt's name, the aunt that was making the pouch for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Weissblatt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she-- that's my mother's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mother's sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the first casualty of the war. During the bombardment, my uncle who owned the store [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was very concerned about what happened if the building got demolished, or it's still standing and if the doors are not broken in. So she volunteered to go and look and check it out. And it-- that's when she got killed. A bomb fell on the building and the whole-- the whole building collapsed. She was buried under the ruins.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was 1939?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Aunt-- Aunt Guta, is that her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guta.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aunt Guta.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your grandmother. What was your grandmother like? Which-- which side of the family was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had only my grandmother from my mother's part of the family. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] She-- I-- I don't remember seeing her being active. She was praying a lot and boring  a lot. But she was elderly, and she-- she didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She had-- well, my mother was the oldest in the family. There were two boys and four girls. My mother was the only one married, except for my uncle in Israel who married too. But all the others waited one   for the other. They waited for the older to get married, because a younger one wouldn't dare getting married before the older sister. So they all got stuck. None of them married. My uncle contacted typhoid fever in the ghetto, and he died from  it. And the rest of the family [SIGHS] was taken to Treblinka, which was a death camp. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I was working for the German railroad at the time. I was a young man. I was 19 years old. And they used to take us every morning. They had some German come in, and we had a Jewish foreman. And they would take us in the morning to the railroad where we worked until late in the afternoon. And then they'll bring us back into the ghetto. They escorted us with a-- a German railroad worker.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day I came home and they were gone. They were picked up, and this was it. Who all was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taken to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this one, at one time, there was my grandmother and my-- and my four aunt. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And a couple of weeks later, my mother and Lilka were picked up. And I haven't seen them anymore. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you told  where they were taken, or did you  know where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nobody to tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father said-- he was very, very distressed. And he-- he tried to get her back by contacting a funeral home to bring her out in a-- in a box. But unfortunately, as soon as they got brought to this place, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: To Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a railroad siding. And they waited there for the big train, the freight trains to come. And unfortunately for my mother, as soon as she was brought to this siding, they put her in the train, into the train, and picked her up right away so she wouldn't be rescued. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you  find out what had happened to her? From your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the only-- the only way. Everybody went the same way to this-- to this particular siding, which was almost in the middle of the city, which-- it was-- you know, it was guarded by the German SS. And-- and there was Jewish police also, which helped the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's-- I want to go back a little bit, because you-- you had already mentioned that you were already in the ghetto. I want to find out from you some of the changes that took place before you were put into the ghetto, like what were some of the first things that happened? Here you're living a wonderful life in Warsaw. Your father had a good business. And what were some of the changes that started happening early on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the-- the first thing was we had to leave the apartment we lived in. It was this section of the town-- this section of Warsaw was not included in the ghetto. So at the beginning, we exchanged apartments. There were Gentiles living in parts of the city which was incorporated into the ghetto and some Jews were living in parts which were out-- excluded from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had to swap the apartments somehow. There was a-- quite a big exchange going on. So we had to move from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember it was-- winter was coming, and we had some coal prepared for the winter. So every time we moved, we had to drag the coal along, which was quite an ordeal, but we had to have it. And we moved about four times, because every time they changed the ghetto, they excluded a street or they incorporated a street, there was big changes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they were establishing the ghetto, I mean, what did you think was happening? Did you think this was a temporary thing? Or what did you think was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the general feeling was that they-- Warsaw is a big city, wouldn't suffer as much as the little towns and the villages, because there was a-- just-- just by the power of the-- of the masses, amount-- number of people living there. They started-- they never-- everything they did was gradually. They did everything gradually. They didn't tell you, they-- we gonna-- gonna kill you, although there were signs all over that the Jews are the source of all the evil, that whatever-- whatever-- whatever went wrong was the Jews' fault.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they started building a wall, and nobody knew. What are they doing? They're building a brick wall in the middle of the city. What are they up to?

speaker: interviewee
text: And little by little, we found out that those are the boundaries of the ghetto. And one day, when the walls were put up, they-- so they said, you all go into the ghetto. Everybody has to move into the ghetto, and you can't go out unless you have a special permission from the Germans. I remember I had a cousin who was quite successful in exporting poultry to Spain, Portugal. And he had the permission to leave the ghetto, to go out on business from the ghetto. And the-- one day, he came to the border, to the gate, and he showed his ID to the gendarme. And he looked at him and says, Herr. You are not a sir. You are a Jew. And he took the ID and tore it into pieces and dropped it on the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no-- no way you could-- you could argue with them. You could plead with them. You were in their hands. And they did-- they made the decisions. We didn't have any-- anything to say about about our own destination, nothing. What were you thinking when-- when you were told that you were going to have to move? Did you-- did your family ever discuss leaving Poland, trying to get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't. You couldn't. They would-- they would take off Jews-- take off the trains. They would take them out from-- from public transportation. We were without any rights. We were not only second-rate citizens, we were no citizens at all. We didn't have anything-- anything whatsoever to-- to-- to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the first things that they made-- because obviously you made a good point that it was slowly. It was gradual. What were some of the first things that happened? You said you saw signs everywhere, and everything that went wrong was the Jews' fault. I mean, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they started with-- the first thing, as soon as they occupied the city, they issued a proclamation that you're not supposed to have any radios, any way of communicating with the outside world. We have to deliver all the radios. You-- you had to deliver all the furs. Jews were not allowed to own furs. And they took, and they had to deposit them in a certain place. That we'll be issued cards, like, to get food. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And the rations were getting smaller and smaller. They were not big to start with. And they started bringing in people from the little hamlets and little villages around Warsaw, and they dump them all into the city. So there was a-- a tremendous hunger and a lot of disease. And people were dying. They were dying on the streets. You could see-- you could see children, especially children, you could see after a while, after a few months, you could see skeleton, living skeletons walking the streets. And you-- you saw them laying on the streets dead, covered with newspapers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing? What was your daily activity like? What sort of things were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was not much we can-- to do. We tried-- everybody tried to make a living. There were people who sold their-- who liquidated their businesses before, and they had this cash. And they didn't know what to do with it, because it just was worthless. It was getting-- so they-- they tried to invest it into something, because if somebody had a shoe store and sold his shoes and had accumulated all this-- this money, and there was no replacement. So he tried to-- so my father did a little business by selling them gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --still had jewelry and gold and-- is that what helped keep you all alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. And then they had to-- when they needed the money to live, they had to sell this gold. So my father did pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this an illegal activity that he was doing? I mean, if  the Germans had found out, would they--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there was no legal activity. We're not-- we're not supposed to do anything. You're not supposed-- I remember one incident. My father and myself and a friend of his were standing on the street and just talking. And a Polish detective took us into a courtyard and said, I want some money from you, for no reason at all. And we had no rights. We had to give him whatever we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people ever try and-- I mean, was there any, I wouldn't say uprising, but did you try and fight back? Were there any little things that you could do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we fought back on different fronts. I mentioned my sister Lilka. She was enrolled in a school, where the Germans forbade all kinds of schooling. There was no schooling at all. And we tried to find-- to fight  back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We organized illegal schools in apartments. We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they organized a library system, whereby people exchanged books without going to a library, because the libraries were closed. We got underground newspapers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were people who had little-- what do you call those presses, by hand, and they distributed it. Everything-- every-- every activity was illegal. There was no legal activity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any information getting in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were-- there were people-- despite the fact that the-- the borderline was always watched over by the Germans, people would smuggle out of the ghetto, get news, get information, and bring it back to the ghetto. That's how we found out in 1942 when they-- when they started this-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the action to bring the people out of the ghetto. This was the Final Solution. And they were trying to get the people not to resist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they told, with proclamations, that the people are being evacuated from Warsaw to the east, where they'll work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] lessen the resistance, they offered three pounds of bread as an incentive, because the hunger was unbelievable. So they offered this here bonus, three pounds of bread, for anybody who will voluntarily go to this assembly place and leave-- leave Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first few days, a lot of people believed them. But then after a few days, word came back that there's no work. There's no [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] any place built there to-- to-- to get to. But it's they're going directly to the death camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people started to resist. They wouldn't go. So they had to use force to get the people to go. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and everybody tried to see any possibility that might-- that might save-- save themselves. OK. We need to stop tape, and we'll pick that up.
